gemma4Tutorial.title: Gemma 4 Tutorial
gemma4Tutorial.intro: Gemma 4 launched on April 2, 2026 in four official sizes: E2B, E4B, 26B A4B, and 31B. The family is built for open-weight deployment under Apache 2.0, with smaller edge models aimed at mobile and laptop-class hardware and larger models aimed at desktops, workstations, and servers.

steps.title: Understand the four official Gemma 4 sizes
steps.description: Gemma 4 comes in E2B, E4B, 26B A4B, and 31B. E2B and E4B accept text, image, and audio input; 26B A4B and 31B accept text and image input and target larger local or server deployments.

steps.title: Match the model to your hardware
steps.description: Use E2B or E4B when you want mobile, edge, or laptop-friendly local inference. Use 26B A4B for a stronger general-purpose local model, and 31B when you want the largest official Gemma 4 checkpoint.

steps.title: Choose a starting point
steps.description: Gemma 4 26B A4B is a strong default for powerful first experiences. If you want the lightest starting point, begin with an instruction-tuned edge model and move up when your workload needs more capability.

steps.title: Pick how you want to try it
steps.description: Try hosted Gemma 4 through Google AI Studio and the Gemini API, or download open weights from Hugging Face or Kaggle for local use, tuning, and custom deployment.

steps.title: Know what Gemma 4 is optimized for
steps.description: The family is built for reasoning, coding, agentic workflows, and multimodal understanding. Edge models support 128K context, while 26B A4B and 31B support up to 256K context.

quickTips: Instruction-tuned (-it) variants are best for chat and assistant use cases.
E2B and E4B are the most hardware-accessible starting points for local experimentation.
The 26B A4B is a Mixture-of-Experts model with faster effective inference than a dense model of similar total size.
All Gemma 4 weights are released under the Apache 2.0 license.

gemma4OllamaSetup.title: Gemma 4 Ollama Setup
gemma4OllamaSetup.intro: Ollama is one of the fastest ways to get Gemma 4 running on a laptop or workstation. The default Ollama flow is simple: install Ollama, pull Gemma 4, confirm the model list, choose the right tag for your hardware, and then run from the CLI or local API.

steps.title: Install and verify Ollama
steps.description: Download Ollama for Windows, macOS, or Linux, install it, and verify the setup with the command ollama --version.

steps.title: Pull the default Gemma 4 variant
steps.description: Use ollama pull gemma4 to download the default Gemma 4 package, then run ollama list to confirm it is available locally.

steps.title: Choose the right model tag
steps.description: Use gemma4:e2b for the lightest edge option, gemma4:e4b for a stronger edge default, gemma4:26b for the 26B A4B MoE workstation model, and gemma4:31b for the full large model.

steps.title: Know what each tag expects
steps.description: On the Ollama library page, e2b is listed at 7.2GB with 128K context, e4b at 9.6GB with 128K, 26b at 18GB with 256K, and 31b at 20GB with 256K.

steps.title: Run your first prompt
steps.description: For a first text test, run ollama run gemma4 "Hello, what can you do?". Ollama also supports image input with the prompt form shown in the official guide.

steps.title: Use the local API for app integration
steps.description: Ollama exposes a local web service at http://localhost:11434/api/generate, so you can move from CLI testing to a lightweight local application without setting up a separate model server.

quickTips: E2B and E4B are the practical first picks for local experimentation on lighter hardware.
The 26b tag targets the 26B A4B MoE model, which uses less active compute than a dense model of similar total size.
ollama list shows all locally downloaded models and their sizes.
Ollama supports image input with the prompt form: ollama run gemma4:e2b with an image path.

gemma4ApiGuide.title: Gemma 4 API Guide
gemma4ApiGuide.intro: The Gemini API provides hosted access to Gemma 4, useful when building without managing local inference. The hosted Gemma 4 models in AI Studio and the Gemini API are gemma-4-26b-a4b-it and gemma-4-31b-it.

steps.title: Create an API key in Google AI Studio
steps.description: Open Google AI Studio and create a Gemini API key. New users can start with a default Google Cloud project, while existing users can import a Cloud project and create keys there.

steps.title: Set the key in your environment
steps.description: The Gemini SDKs automatically pick up GEMINI_API_KEY or GOOGLE_API_KEY. If both are set, GOOGLE_API_KEY takes precedence.

steps.title: Install the official SDK
steps.description: For Python, install google-genai. For JavaScript and TypeScript, install @google/genai. Google also publishes SDK paths for Go, Java, C#, and Apps Script.

steps.title: Choose the hosted Gemma 4 model ID
steps.description: For hosted Gemma 4, use gemma-4-26b-a4b-it for a faster MoE large model, or gemma-4-31b-it for the flagship dense checkpoint.

steps.title: Send a first generateContent request
steps.description: The official example uses client.models.generate_content with the model field set to gemma-4-31b-it. In REST, requests go to the generateContent endpoint with the x-goog-api-key header.

steps.title: Use AI Studio to bridge from testing to code
steps.description: Google AI Studio lets you experiment with prompts, model settings, function calling, and structured output, then export working code through the Get code flow.

quickTips: AI Studio is the fastest way to test Gemma 4 prompts before writing any code.
The Gemini API supports streaming responses for chat and long-generation use cases.
gemma-4-26b-a4b-it is the MoE model — generally faster and more cost-efficient than 31B.
Function calling and structured output are available for both hosted Gemma 4 model IDs.

gemma4HuggingFaceDownload.title: Gemma 4 Hugging Face Download
gemma4HuggingFaceDownload.intro: The official Google collection on Hugging Face includes eight core Gemma 4 checkpoints: E2B, E4B, 26B A4B, and 31B, each in base and instruction-tuned form. Instruction-tuned (-it) repositories are the natural starting point for chat, coding, and assistant experiences.

cards.name: google/gemma-4-E2B-it
cards.description: Edge checkpoint with text, image, and audio input and 128K context. Best for fast local assistants and on-device multimodal experimentation.

cards.name: google/gemma-4-E4B-it
cards.description: Stronger edge checkpoint with text, image, and audio input and 128K context. More capable than E2B without jumping to workstation-class hardware.

cards.name: google/gemma-4-26B-A4B-it
cards.description: Mixture-of-Experts checkpoint with 256K context and text-image input. Large-model quality with faster effective inference than a dense model of similar total size.

cards.name: google/gemma-4-31B-it
cards.description: Flagship dense Gemma 4 checkpoint with 256K context and text-image input. Best for the strongest chat, reasoning, coding, and agent workflows.

cards.name: google/gemma-4-E2B
cards.description: Base edge checkpoint for users who want to study, adapt, or fine-tune the smallest multimodal Gemma 4 model.

cards.name: google/gemma-4-E4B
cards.description: Base edge checkpoint that keeps text, image, and audio input while leaving downstream instruction behavior to your own tuning pipeline.

cards.name: google/gemma-4-26B-A4B
cards.description: Base MoE large checkpoint for custom adaptation where you want the 26B A4B architecture without default instruction-tuned behavior.

cards.name: google/gemma-4-31B
cards.description: Base 31B dense checkpoint for teams that want the largest official Gemma 4 foundation model before their own fine-tuning or alignment stage.

faq.title: Frequently Asked
faq.subtitle: Everything you need to know about Gemma 4

questions.question: What is Gemma 4?
questions.answer: Gemma 4 is Google DeepMind's newest open model family released on April 2, 2026. It comes in four sizes — E2B, E4B, 26B A4B, and 31B — and supports text, image, and audio inputs with up to 256K context under the Apache 2.0 license.

questions.question: What are the Gemma 4 model sizes?
questions.answer: Gemma 4 has four sizes: E2B (edge, ~2B params), E4B (edge, ~4B params), 26B A4B (Mixture-of-Experts, 26B total/4B active), and 31B (dense). E2B and E4B support audio input and target mobile and laptop hardware; 26B A4B and 31B target workstations and servers.

questions.question: How do I run Gemma 4 locally?
questions.answer: The most common way is through Ollama. Install Ollama, run ollama pull gemma4 for the default variant, then use ollama run gemma4 to start chatting. For a lighter option, try gemma4:e2b or gemma4:e4b. LM Studio is another popular choice for a GUI-based local setup.

questions.question: What is the Gemma 4 context window?
questions.answer: Gemma 4 E2B and E4B support a 128K context window. Gemma 4 26B A4B and 31B support up to 256K context, making them suitable for long documents, extended conversations, and large codebase analysis.

questions.question: How is Gemma 4 26B A4B different from Gemma 4 31B?
questions.answer: Gemma 4 26B A4B is a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model with 26B total parameters but only ~4B active per token. This means faster inference and lower memory usage compared to the 31B dense model, while still achieving strong performance on benchmarks.

questions.question: Can I use Gemma 4 through an API without local setup?
questions.answer: Yes. Gemma 4 is available through the Gemini API and Google AI Studio as gemma-4-26b-a4b-it and gemma-4-31b-it. Create an API key in AI Studio, install the google-genai SDK, and start sending requests without any local model management.
